era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Amenhotep III. The Hittite Empire is expanding under Suppiluliuma I. Mycenaean Greece is in the Late Helladic IIIA period. The Shang Dynasty in China is in its later phases (c. 1250–1050 BCE, though dating is debated). This is an era of international diplomacy, monumental construction, and trade networks spanning from Egypt to the Aegean to Anatolia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (oracle bone records are fragmentary and difficult to date precisely)
South Asia (Indus Valley civilization has declined; Vedic period is poorly dated)

events.title: Amenhotep III's jubilee festival (Heb Sed) preparations
events.description: Amenhotep III, now in his 30th regnal year, is preparing for or celebrating a jubilee festival (Heb Sed). This festival, traditionally held after 30 years of rule, was a ritual renewal of the pharaoh's divine power and legitimacy. The festival involved elaborate ceremonies, the construction of temporary structures, and the participation of priests and officials from throughout Egypt. The festival reinforced the pharaoh's connection to the gods and his role as maintainer of cosmic order (ma'at).

sources.name: Temple inscriptions and reliefs at Thebes and other sites

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III is known to have celebrated a jubilee festival. The exact year is uncertain, but it likely occurred around year 30 of his reign, which would place it c. 1360 BCE (±5 years).

events.title: Hittite consolidation of power in Anatolia and the Levant
events.description: Suppiluliuma I continues to consolidate Hittite power in Anatolia and expand influence into the Levant. The Hittites are establishing vassal relationships with city-states and kingdoms in Syria and northern Mesopotamia. This expansion brings the Hittites into closer contact and competition with Egypt for control of the Levant. The period is marked by military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions mentioning Hittite conflicts

events.certainty_note: Suppiluliuma I's reign is dated c. 1344–1322 BCE. His expansion into the Levant is well-attested, but specific events in -1382 are inferred from the broader pattern of his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Suppiluliuma I's reign is debated. Some scholars place it c. 1344–1322 BCE (standard), while others argue for dates shifted by 20–50 years. The Hittite chronology remains one of the most contested areas of ancient Near Eastern chronology. No disconfirming evidence undermines the events listed, but their precise dating is uncertain.
historiographic_note: The Late Bronze Age is well-documented through Egyptian inscriptions, Hittite archives, and archaeological evidence. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. The Hittite perspective is known through royal archives; the Egyptian perspective through temple inscriptions. The viewpoints of vassal states and common people are largely absent from the surviving record.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Amenhotep III's reign and building programme
Hittite expansion that eventually led to direct conflict with Egypt
